title: Surface Area of a Hemisphere Calculator
tagline: Calculate the curved surface area, base area, and total surface area of a hemisphere from its radius. Instant results with all three formulas.
intro: Enter the radius of the hemisphere and the calculator returns the curved surface area (2πr²), base circle area (πr²), and total surface area (3πr²).

error.invalid: Please enter a valid positive number for the radius.

about.heading: About the hemisphere surface area calculator
about.body:
A hemisphere is exactly half of a sphere — the solid you get when you slice a sphere through its center with a flat plane. It has two surfaces: the curved dome (half the sphere's outer surface) and the flat circular base. The surface area of a hemisphere calculator computes all three quantities you might need: the curved surface area, the base circle area, and the total surface area that encloses the solid entirely.

The curved surface area of a hemisphere is half the surface area of the full sphere. Since a complete sphere has a surface area of 4πr², the curved half equals 2πr². This is the area you would need to cover if you were painting or cladding the outside of a dome without the flat floor.

The base circle area is simply the area of the circular cross-section created by the cut: πr². This is the floor of the hemisphere — needed for example when you want to tile the bottom of a hemispherical bowl or calculate the material needed to seal the flat face.

The total surface area is the sum of both: curved surface area plus base area = 2πr² + πr² = 3πr². Use this figure when you need the complete outer envelope of the solid — for instance, when estimating the amount of material needed to manufacture a hemispherical container closed on both sides.

Hemispherical shapes appear throughout architecture, engineering, and everyday life. Geodesic domes and sports arenas often use hemispherical or near-hemispherical profiles. Bowls, cups, satellite dishes, and the pressurized end caps of industrial tanks are frequently hemispherical. In physics, the hemispherical surface integral is used in radiometry and antenna theory to integrate radiation patterns over a half-space.

For precise calculations, remember that the radius is measured from the center of the flat base to any point on the curved surface — it is the same as the radius of the original sphere. The calculator accepts any positive number, including decimals, so you can work with millimetres, centimetres, metres, or any other unit. The three output values are in square units — the square of whatever unit you entered for the radius.

examples.heading: Hemisphere surface area examples
examples.intro: Four examples with different radii showing all three surface area values.

items.note: Curved = 2π×20.25 ≈ 127.23 mm²; Base = π×20.25 ≈ 63.62 mm²; Total ≈ 190.85 mm².

howto.heading: How to use the hemisphere surface area calculator
steps: Enter the radius of the hemisphere in the Radius (r) field. Use any positive number — decimals are accepted.
Click Calculate. The calculator instantly shows all three values: curved surface area (2πr²), base circle area (πr²), and total surface area (3πr²).
Use the example buttons to load a pre-set radius and see worked results.
Click Reset to clear the field and start a new calculation.

faq.heading: Hemisphere surface area FAQ
faq.q1: What is the difference between curved surface area and total surface area of a hemisphere?
faq.q1_a: The curved surface area (2πr²) covers only the dome — the rounded half-sphere portion. The total surface area (3πr²) adds the flat circular base (πr²) to give the complete outer surface of the closed solid. Use curved surface area for open-top bowls or domes; use total surface area for fully enclosed hemispherical containers.
faq.q2: How is the hemisphere surface area related to a full sphere?
faq.q2_a: A full sphere has a surface area of 4πr². The curved surface of a hemisphere is exactly half of that: 2πr². However, the total surface area of a hemisphere (3πr²) is three-quarters of a full sphere's surface area, because the base adds an extra πr².
faq.q3: Does the formula change if the hemisphere is hollow?
faq.q3_a: The formulas given (2πr² for curved, πr² for base, 3πr² for total) apply to the outer surface of a solid or thin-walled hemisphere. If the hemisphere is hollow with wall thickness t, you must calculate both outer and inner surfaces separately and add them. For a thin shell (where t is negligible compared to r), the formulas above are a good approximation.
faq.q4: How do I find the radius if I know the total surface area?
faq.q4_a: Rearrange 3πr² = SA to get r = √(SA / (3π)). For example, if the total surface area is 942.48 square units, then r = √(942.48 / 9.4248) = √100 = 10 units. The calculator works in the forward direction (r → SA), but you can invert the formula manually.
faq.q5_a: You can use any unit — centimetres, metres, inches, or feet — as long as the radius is measured in that unit. The surface area will then be in square units of the same type. There is no unit conversion in the calculator, so consistency is essential.
faq.q6: Is the hemisphere surface area formula used in real engineering?
faq.q6_a: Yes. Hemispherical end caps on pressure vessels, fuel tanks, and pharmaceutical bioreactors are designed using these formulas to calculate wall area, material costs, and structural load. Satellite dish installers and dome architects also use hemispherical surface area to estimate cladding material and heat-transfer characteristics.
